title: practical english usage - Part 185
content:
Who can be used as an object in identifying clauses in an informal style.

3 who     and   whom I do a lot of walking which keeps me fit. (= ... and this keeps me fit.) the floor. (= ... and it knocked ... and they went...) I dropped the saucepan, which knocked over the eggs, which went all over it on ...) She passed the bill to Moriarty, who passed it on to me. (= ... and he passed general-purpose connecting words, rather like and + pronoun.

In non-identifying clauses, the pronouns who and which sometimes act as 2 relative      pronouns       as  general-purpose           connectors nouns or pronouns in other clauses - rather like conjunctions.

inside relative clauses, and at the same time they connect relative clauses to Note that relative pronouns have a double use. They  act as subjects or objects 1  double      use   of  relative      pronouns 237 relatives:      advanced       points For   sentences        like    What / want is a rest (’ cleft    sentences*),       ►  274.

I’ll give you what help 1 can. (=... any help that I can.) he has ...) What money he has comes from his family. (= The  little money that like 'the only’ , 'the little’  or ‘ the few’ .

What can also be used as a determiner with a noun. It often means something 3 what      as  a  determiner:       what      money        he   has not (      ...

Sophie married Joe, which made Oliver unhappy,    what-made-...) We   use which, not what, to refer to a whole clause that comes before. (►   233.8) not ( The only thing that keeps me awake is coffee,   The only-thing what...) not (      ...

We haven't got everything that you ordered,    everything what...) relative pronoun after a noun or pronoun.

What is only used to mean 'the thing(s) which'. It cannot be used as an ordinary 2 what      not   used For   singular       and    plural     verbs     after    what (e.g.   What we need most is/are books), ►  130.5.

This is exactly what I wanted, (complement) I hope you're going to give me what I need, (object of give) What she said made me angry, (subject of made) can act as subjects, objects, or complements after be.

pronoun together, and means 'the thing(s) which*. Clauses beginning with what What does not refer to a noun that comes before it. It acts as noun + relative 1  meaning       and   use:  the    thing(s)      which 236 what grammar •  237   relatives: advanced points we-ean-stay for a few days.)                                                                            H but  not ( We need a place (that) we can stay for a few days,    We need a-house Have you got somewhere (that) I can lie down for an hour?

nowhere and place (but not after other words).

The  same thing happens with where after somewhere, anywhere, everywhere, That was the year (that) I first went abroad.

I'll never forget the day (that) we met.

Come and see us any time (that) you’re in town.

dropped in an informal style.

After common nouns referring to time, when is often replaced by that or 7 when, where, etc   replaced      by  that or  dropped He spoke in Greek, which language I could only follow with difficulty.

He was appointed Lord Chancellor, in which post he spent the rest of his life.

He lost his temper, at which point I decided to go home.

She may be late, in which case we ought to wait for her.

in which case and at which point.

and is mainly used after prepositions, especially in some fixed phrases like which  repeats the meaning of what came before. This structure is rather formal, Which can be used as a determiner in relative clauses, with a general noun 6  which as  determiner;        in which case a more formal style) the people that live next door (informal; the people who... is preferred in the bus that crashed (normal) the people that I invited (normal)   the books that I lent you (normal) Compare: That can be used as a subject instead of who, but this is quite informal.

(►   233.3). That is most common as an object, or as a subject instead of which.

That is often used in identifying relative clauses instead of who!whom/which 5 that for   people students in the USA might be expected to follow it.
